key_points: Markets connect producers and consumers through systems of buying and selling
Different types of markets serve different needs such as weekly markets, neighbourhood markets, malls, and wholesale markets
Weekly markets allow small traders to sell goods without paying high shop rent
Neighbourhood shops provide convenience and often allow customers to buy on credit
The chain of markets helps goods move from producers to consumers through wholesalers and retailers
examples: A farmer selling vegetables to a wholesaler who supplies retail shops
Weekly markets where traders sell fruits, clothes, utensils, and other goods
Neighbourhood grocery shops that provide daily household items
Shopping malls selling branded products at fixed prices
Street vendors selling snacks and fruits directly to consumers
formulas: Producer → Person who makes or grows goods
Wholesaler → Buys goods in large quantities and sells to retailers
Retailer → Sells goods directly to consumers
Market Chain → Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer
exam_tips: Learn the role of wholesalers and retailers in the market chain
Understand the advantages and disadvantages of weekly markets and malls
Remember examples that explain how goods reach consumers
Focus on how neighbourhood markets help in daily life
